1:
The chapter opens on an act of courtesy that will end in war, and the witnesses read it at two levels — the human occasion and the shadow it casts forward. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the facts. Charles Ellicott supplies the name the verse withholds until v.2 and 1 Chron. 19:1 — Nahash, probably a son or grandson of the Nahash whom Saul had routed more than fifty years before (1 Sam. 11) — and guesses that the unrecorded kindness may have been some friendly help during David's wandering years, or merely a congratulatory embassy on his accession. John Gill sets the chronology, noting that the notice of Ammonite spoils in 2 Sam. 8:12 is written by anticipation, for those spoils were not taken until this very war.

Keil and Delitzsch lift the eye to the whole arc of the reign. In its first half David had strengthened and fortified the kingdom until all its enemies bowed; but from this Ammonite campaign onward a series of heavy judgments falls upon his house, for it was during this war, while Joab besieged Rabbah and David lingered in Jerusalem (ch. 11:1), that he stained his soul with adultery and murder, falling all the deeper because of the height to which God had raised him. The bright victory of this chapter is thus the last unclouded page before the shadow.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the grace of gratitude. Nahash had been a cruel enemy of Israel (1 Sam. 11:2), yet had shown kindness to David — perhaps only in spite of Saul — and David, having lately tasted the pleasure of requiting Jonathan's love to Mephibosheth, resolves to return it: he showed kindness to me. Henry draws the tender lesson that it is a comfort to children, when their parents are dead, to find that their parents' friends are theirs, and a comfort to mourners to find those who will mourn with them. If David receives kindness, he is not nice in examining the grounds and principles of it, but resolves gratefully to return it — for God knows the heart, but we do not. The witnesses agree that the embassy was well meant; where they will divide, in the verses to come, is over how a courtesy so plainly kind could be so darkly misread.
2:
David's own words give the verse its heart — I will show kindness unto Hanun the son of Nahash, as his father showed kindness unto me — and the witnesses gather round the motive and the courtesy of condolence. John Gill reads it simply as a friendly compliment of sympathy on a father's death, and John Wesley confirms that there had hitherto been genuine friendship between the two kings, which is why the spoils of Ammon are mentioned by anticipation in ch. 8:12 rather than in their place.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke probes the obligation David felt. We do not know its nature or extent, he admits, but conjectures that this Nahash was the very king who had once besieged Jabesh-gilead and been defeated by Saul; since David had taken refuge with the neighbouring Moabites (1 Sam. 22:3), and since Nahash's hatred of Saul might well have inclined him to befriend Saul's rival, the kindness may have sprung from the old maxim that an enemy's enemy is a friend. Keil and Delitzsch keep the narrative moving, translating the embassy as David sending to comfort him by the hand of his servants, and already setting the trap that the Ammonite princes will spring in the next verse.

It is the kind of gratitude, the commentators imply, that asks few questions: a favour once received creates an obligation the generous heart is glad to own, and David — newly warmed by his own kindness to the house of Saul in the person of Mephibosheth — is quick to look for a way to repay Ammon in kind. Underneath the differing guesses at the history the schools are one on the character of the act. David is doing what princes in alliance do, sending comforters to a bereaved son, and doing it out of a gratitude that will not weigh too narrowly the reasons a kindness was first shown him. It is the very transparency of the gesture that makes the coming insult so gross: a hand held out in friendship is about to be seized and shamed. The witnesses let the verse stand as a small monument to a generous impulse, and turn, with something like foreboding, to the suspicious counsel that will pervert it.
3:
The hinge of the tragedy is the whisper of the Ammonite princes: Thinkest thou that David doth honour thy father...? hath he not rather sent his servants to search the city, and to spy it out, and to overthrow it? The witnesses read it as a study in how a suspicious heart poisons a plain kindness. Charles Ellicott gives the strategic ground of the fear: the city in question is Rabbah, the strongly fortified capital and almost the only city of Ammon, whose interior a foreign power would dearly love to know; and the princes' alarm, he suggests, had been roused by David's growing power and his recent conquest of their Moabite neighbours. Keil and Delitzsch weigh the very grammar of the taunt, noting that the first question is framed to expect the answer 'no' and the second, differently introduced, to press for a 'yes' — a rhetoric built to manufacture certainty out of nothing.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns the verse into a maxim on courts. It has been a just complaint through all the history of mankind, he says, that there is little sincerity in palaces: courtiers, conscious of their own insincerity, are quick to suspect it in others, and so mislead their sovereigns. John Gill simply identifies the speakers as the nobles and prime ministers, the courtiers about the young king — the men best placed to steer him wrong.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the moral hardest, and it is his phrases the tradition remembers. False men, he observes, are ready to think others as false as themselves, and those who bear ill-will to their neighbours are resolved not to believe that their neighbours bear any good-will to them: unfounded suspicion argues a wicked mind. They would never have imagined David dissembled but that they were conscious they could have dissembled themselves, to serve a turn. With Bishop Patrick he adds that there is nothing so well meant but it may be ill interpreted, and is wont to be so by men who love nobody but themselves; and he warns that men of the greatest honour must not think it strange to be thus misrepresented, for charity thinketh no evil (1 Cor. 13:5). The voices do not divide: with one mind they trace the coming war not to David's design but to the self-projection of a guilty imagination.
4:
Hanun, persuaded, heaps on David's envoys the extremest insult the East could devise: he shaves off half of each man's beard and cuts away their garments to the buttocks. The witnesses labour to make a modern reader feel the outrage. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the cultural weight. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott both cite the traveller Arvieux: cutting off a person's beard is regarded by the Arabs as an indignity quite equal to flogging and branding among ourselves; many would rather die than have their beard shaved off. K&D adds Niebuhr's report of a Persian prince who in 1764 so mutilated an embassy and provoked a war — a near-exact parallel to the scene before us. Ellicott draws the larger observation that runs through the book: in none of David's wars does he appear as the aggressor.

The wesleyan-arminian voices explain why the double insult cut so deep. Adam Clarke notes that the beard was the possessor's greatest ornament, sworn by and even pledged in weighty matters, and never cut off except in mourning or as the badge of a slave; to shave half of it and slash the robe was to render free men ridiculous and to brand them as bondmen — an accumulation of insult. John Wesley is blunter still on the second half: the cutting of the garments in the midst was worse than the shaving, for the Israelites wore no breeches, so that their nakedness was thereby uncovered.

John Gill keeps the plain sense — these were David's ambassadors, men under the special protection that all nations owe an envoy — and the horror of the act lies precisely there. The messengers had come unarmed, trusting the Ammonites' hospitality, on an errand of comfort; they are sent home half-shorn, half-naked, and made a spectacle. The witnesses do not debate the meaning; they only pile up the evidence of how deliberate and how deadly the affront was, an act that, by the law of nations, was itself a declaration of war. What remains is to watch how David, and then God, will answer it.
5:
David's response to his shamed servants is all tenderness, and the witnesses dwell on the delicacy of it. Keil and Delitzsch render the king's motive exactly: hearing of the insult heaped on his men, David sent messengers to meet them and bid them remain at Jericho until their beards were grown again, that he might not have to set his eyes upon the insult they had received. The consideration is for the men, not for the king's dignity. Charles Ellicott notes that Jericho lay directly on their road home, so the kindness cost them no detour, and he pauses over the town's strange history — destroyed at the first entrance into Canaan under a solemn curse on whoever should rebuild it, a curse that fell on Hiel a century after David (1 Kings 16:34) — yet, since build there means fortify, Jericho was all along an inhabited place, the city of palm trees of Judg. 3:13. Adam Clarke agrees that though unwalled since Joshua's day the site kept its cottages, and being comparatively private it spared the envoys the public gaze.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry mines the verse for a lesson on injured reputation. The Jews wore their beards long, reckoning it an honour to appear aged and grave, so that men of rank could not decently appear at court unlike their neighbours; their change of raiment could be repaired at once, but the lost beard needed time. And time, Henry says, is exactly the remedy for slander: let us learn not to lay too much to heart unjust reproaches; after a while they will wear off of themselves, and turn only to the shame of their authors, while the injured reputation in a little time grows again, as these beards did. He clinches it with the promise that God will bring forth our righteousness as the light, and bids us wait patiently for him (Ps. 37:6).

Henry alone adds a probing afterthought — that David was perhaps but well served for courting a pagan prince he knew to be an inveterate enemy of Israel, a people with whom an Ammonite might not so much as enter the congregation of the Lord to the tenth generation (Deut. 23:3). The others do not press the point; together the witnesses leave the verse as a picture of a king who feels his servants' disgrace as his own and quietly shields them until it can heal.
6:
Waking too late to what they have done, the Ammonites saw that they stank before David and, instead of seeking peace, buy an army. The witnesses handle the idiom, the sums, and the folly. Adam Clarke and Charles Ellicott gloss the Hebrew phrase — literally that they had made themselves stink, become abominable to David by their own act (compare Gen. 34:30). Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott then tally the mercenaries: twenty thousand foot from Aram of Beth-rehob and Zobah, a thousand from the king of Maacah, and twelve thousand men of Tob, hired, as 1 Chron. 19:6 records, for a thousand talents of silver — nearly half a million pounds, a sum that shows at once the wealth of Ammon and the gravity of the war. Clarke maps the petty kingdoms — Beth-rehob at the head of the valley between Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon, Zobah subject to Hadadezer, Maacah near Hermon, Ish-tob the land where Jephthah once took refuge (Judg. 11:3) — while Ellicott adds from Chronicles that the allies mustered at Medeba on the Reubenite plain (Josh. 13:9).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the muster into a parable of impenitence. The Ammonites had made themselves odious and obnoxious to David's just displeasure, and had they only humbled themselves an honorary satisfaction might have atoned for the offence; but, desperately resolved to stand by what they had done, they courted their own ruin. So sinners daringly provoke God and raise forces to justify their first defiance, never considering that he is stronger than they (1 Cor. 10:22). It is a note the whole tradition sounds: the second sin, the arming, is worse than the first, the affront, because it is the refusal to repent of it.

John Gill states the plain psychology — knowing David incensed, they neither appeased him nor gave satisfaction but bought swords — and so the verse becomes the pivot on which a diplomatic insult swells into a coalition war. The witnesses do not divide over the history; with one voice they read Ammon's hired thousands as the fruit of a pride that would rather ruin itself than confess a wrong.
7:
The verse is brief — David hears of the muster and sends Joab with all the host of the mighty men — and the witnesses read it for David's promptness and for the phrase describing his troops. Keil and Delitzsch parse the grammar, taking the mighty men as standing in apposition to all the host: not a select corps beside the army but the whole army itself, the brave and battle-hardened people of Israel. Adam Clarke reads it the same way — all his worthies, and the flower of his army — so that David meets the coalition with his full strength rather than a token force.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a window on the whole strategy and on its spiritual counterpart. It was David's prudence, he observes, to carry the war into the enemy's country and fight them at the very gate of their city, for every good prince, in love to his people, keeps the miseries of war as far as may be from their homes. But Henry hears a deeper music: those who are at war with the Son of David not only give the provocation but begin the war, for he waits to be gracious; and if they will not turn, he will whet his sword (Ps. 7:12). God has forces to send against those who set his wrath at defiance (Isa. 5:19), forces that will convince them, too late, that none ever hardened his heart against God and prospered.

John Gill supplies only the plain occasion — David heard of the preparation the Ammonites had made — but the plainness is the point: the king does not wait to be attacked at home. The witnesses agree that the swift dispatch of Joab and the whole army is at once sound generalship and a figure of the certain answer that provoked justice will make. Ammon and Aram have raised the sword; Israel's mighty men are already on the march to meet it, and the stage is set for the double battle that the next verses describe.
8:
The two hostile forces take up their positions, and the witnesses attend to the topography that will decide the fight. Keil and Delitzsch describe it precisely: the Ammonites came out from their capital and drew up in battle array before the gate, while the hired Syrians stood alone in the field, taking a separate station on the broad, treeless table-land (compare Josh. 13:16) by Medeba, some four hours' march south-west of Rabbath-Ammon. Adam Clarke, following 1 Chron. 19:7, identifies the gate as that of Medeba, the frontier town before which the allies had encamped. Charles Ellicott reads the disposition as deliberate: the Ammonites and their mercenaries formed separate armies, the townsmen posted immediately before the city, the Syrians at a distance where the open ground gave room for the manoeuvres of their chariots.

John Gill weighs the small geographical puzzle — whether the Ammonites issued from Rabbah, as Josephus supposed, or from Medeba, as the parallel in Chronicles suggests — and settles, with the others, on the border city. The point the witnesses care about is not which gate but the shape of the enemy line, for it is that shape which puts Israel in danger. By stationing the Ammonites at the gate behind Joab and the Syrians on the plain before him, the coalition has caught the army of Israel between two fronts, able to be charged in the flank and rear at once while it faces the enemy ahead. It was, the commentators recognise, a well-laid snare, and the sort of position in which a lesser commander than Joab might have been enveloped and destroyed.

There is no dispute here; the verse is the still moment before the clash, and the commentators are content to draw the map. Yet in drawing it they set up the crisis of the chapter — the peril of a divided front — and prepare the reader for the swift, cool generalship of v.9, where Joab, seeing the trap, turns it against those who laid it. The Ammonites had chosen their ground to encircle; they will find that the ground has been turned into a place where their two armies can be beaten one after another, and neither can rescue the other.
9:
Joab's soldier's eye takes in the danger at a glance, and the witnesses admire the coolness of his response. John Gill renders the peril literally — the face of the battle was against him before and behind, armies both ahead and at his back, ready to strike him in front and rear at once. Adam Clarke reconstructs how it happened: one hostile army stood in the open field and the other within the city, so that when Joab fronted the first, the second issued from the gate and took him from behind, forcing him to split his force. Keil and Delitzsch describe the remedy — Joab chose of all the choice men of Israel and drew them up against the Syrians, giving the rest to his brother Abishai against the Ammonites.

Charles Ellicott most admires the generalship. The keen eye of this experienced commander, he writes, at once took in both the advantage and the danger of the enemy's disposition: Joab threw his whole force between their two divisions, organising his own army in two parts, each facing one foe yet each able to support the other, so that the enemy was cut in two, while the Israelites formed one compact body. And he read the field shrewdly, keeping the choice troops and the harder task — the stronger Syrian mercenaries — under his own hand, while committing the Ammonites to Abishai.

The witnesses do not divide; they concur that the moment which looked like Israel's undoing became, in a competent hand, its opportunity. A double front is deadly only to an army that panics; met by a general who divides his men without dividing their purpose, it becomes two separate battles, each winnable in turn. The tradition lets the verse stand as a small textbook of nerve and order under pressure, and hurries on to the compact of mutual help between the brothers, and to the noble speech in which Joab will lift the whole enterprise from tactics to trust in God. Human wisdom sets the array; the next verses will show where Joab thought the victory truly lay.
10:
The verse records only that Joab delivered the rest of the people into the hand of his brother Abishai, to be arrayed against the children of Ammon, and the witnesses read it as the completing half of the wise division begun in v.9. John Gill supplies what the terse notice implies: Abishai was a commander under Joab and a very valiant man, so that as the enemy had marshalled two armies, Israel now met them with two, the better to attack them both. Abishai's courage was no untried thing; he had stood at David's side in desperate straits before, and his brother now trusts him with an entire wing of the army. Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward, joining this verse to the speech that follows as the setting for Joab's charge to his brother.

The point the commentators draw out is the prudence of the arrangement. Joab has not weakened his army by dividing it but doubled its reach, matching front to front; and, crucially, he has put the harder assignment — the seasoned Syrian mercenaries — on himself and the choicest men, leaving the townsmen of Ammon, who could always retreat behind their walls, to Abishai. It is a distribution that reckons honestly with the strength of each foe and spares his brother the heavier blow.

Yet the true genius of the plan lies not in the splitting of the force but in what binds the two halves together, which the next verse will state: a pledge that neither wing shall fight for itself alone. The witnesses treat this verse as the quiet hinge between the danger seen in v.9 and the courage spoken in v.12. Abishai takes his post; the line is set; and the two brothers, each with a host and a common enemy, stand ready to prove that mutual support, not mere numbers, is what will carry the day. What might have been two isolated commands, each liable to be overwhelmed, becomes under Joab's compact a single field with two faces, and the God whose cities they defend will be asked to give the increase.
11:
Joab's compact with his brother is the soul of the plan: If the Syrians be too strong for me, then thou shalt help me; but if the children of Ammon be too strong for thee, then I will come and help thee. The witnesses, though few here, read it as a model of brotherly generalship and of Christian fellowship in a common cause. John Gill attends to the practical mechanics — the two wings must have agreed on a signal, so that each could tell when the other was pressed and giving way, the moment for the promised relief; the arrangement supposes that neither brother will grasp all the glory, but that each will run to the other's danger.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the verse a little sermon on mutual aid. Joab prudently concerts the matter with Abishai so that the dividing of the forces should not be the weakening of them, supposing the worst — that one wing may be forced to give back — and providing that in that case the other shall send a timely detachment to its rescue. From this he draws the rule that mutual helpfulness is brotherly duty: as the members of the natural body help one another (1 Cor. 12:21), so Christ's soldiers should strengthen one another's hands in their spiritual warfare, the strong succouring the weak. Those who through grace are conquerors over temptation must counsel, comfort, and pray for those who are tempted; and Henry hears behind the verse the Lord's word to Peter, when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren (Luke 22:32).

There is no debate to record; the two voices agree, and the verse's very brevity concentrates its lesson. Joab plans for the possibility of his own weakness, not out of fear but out of foresight, and ties his fortune to his brother's. The witnesses leave it as a picture of how those engaged in one warfare, under one Lord, ought to stand — each ready to break off from his own success to relieve another's distress, so that the whole line holds where a single wing might have broken. What Joab arranges between himself and Abishai, grace arranges among the servants of the Son of David.
12:
Here the battle-speech reaches its height: Be of good courage, and let us play the men for our people, and for the cities of our God: and the LORD do that which seemeth him good. The witnesses prize it as one of the noblest utterances in the historical books, and read it in two movements — brave resolve and pious resignation. Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew more literally, Be strong, and let us strengthen ourselves (the Chronicler's let us behave ourselves valiantly, compare 1 Sam. 4:9), and both explain the striking phrase the cities of our God: the whole land was Jehovah's own possession, lent to Israel, so that Joab and Abishai fight lest God's cities fall into the hands of the heathen and become subject to their gods.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke calls it a very fine military address, equal to anything in ancient or modern times: they fight pro aris et focis, for altars and hearths, for everything sacred and civil — for God, for their families, and for their country. John Gill notes the pastoral wisdom of the timing, for Joab spoke in the hearing of the officers and people, who might well be daunted by the enemy's numbers and by the peril of being hemmed in before and behind, and needed heartening before the charge.

Yet the witnesses love the speech most for its close. Matthew Henry and Ellicott both dwell on the LORD do that which seemeth him good — rather, as Ellicott renders it, the LORD will do — in which Joab's courage rises to its highest point: the full trust that, whatever the result, it will be that which seems best to infinite wisdom and love. Henry frames the balance the whole tradition finds here: when we have done our part according to the duty of our place, we may leave the issue with God; let nothing be wanting in us, whatever the success be; let God's work be done by us, and then God's will be done concerning us. Valour does not bind God to prosper us, but neither does trust excuse us from valour. The voices do not divide: with one accord they hold together the two things Joab holds — that we must strive as if all depended on our strength, and yield as knowing all depends on God's good pleasure.
13:
The plan is executed and the first result comes at once: Joab and his people drew near to the battle against the Syrians, and they fled before him. The witnesses admire both the tactic and its speed. John Gill explains the reasoning: Joab fell upon the Syrians first, rightly judging that hired soldiers, once hard pressed, would give way, and that their flight would dishearten the Ammonites who leaned upon them; and, following Josephus, he adds that after a brief sharp struggle Joab killed many and put the rest to flight. Charles Ellicott reads the same wisdom in it — the attack was launched not against both foes at once but with the full weight thrown against the stronger division, while Abishai held the Ammonites in check — and pronounces the tactics completely successful.

Keil and Delitzsch state the outcome in the barest terms, that Joab advanced against Aram and they fled before him, letting the plain fact carry its own force. The mercenaries who had been bought with a thousand talents proved, when the battle turned hot, to have no heart for a quarrel not their own; the first firm charge scattered them. It is a lesson the older commentators do not fail to press: hired valour is brittle, and an army bound to a cause only by wages will not stand where men fighting for their homes would die in their ranks.

Behind the military notice the puritan-evangelical reading of the chapter, drawn out by Matthew Henry, finds its confirmation: Joab had provided for the worst, supposing in v.11 that either foe might prove too strong, and yet he proved too strong for both — for, Henry observes, we do not hinder our success by preparing for disappointment. The wing that braced itself against possible defeat won the swifter victory. The witnesses agree without dispute: the crisis of the double front, so carefully met with divided ranks and a pledge of mutual aid, dissolves the moment battle is joined, because the coalition's strength was rented and its heart was not in it. What remains is to see the Ammonites, their hired shield broken, do exactly what Joab foresaw.
14:
The Syrians' collapse decides the Ammonites, and the witnesses read the verse for the psychology of the rout and for the puzzle of Joab's withdrawal. Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch narrate the flight plainly: when the children of Ammon saw that the Syrians, in whom they had trusted, were fled, they too fled before Abishai and drew back within the city, whereupon Joab broke off and returned to Jerusalem. John Gill underlines the point of dependence — it was the flight of the hired troops in whom they put great confidence that unnerved them — so that the Ammonites seem hardly to have fought at all, but on seeing their mercenaries broken simply retired behind their walls. The prop they had bought with silver gave way, and their own courage, which had never been more than borrowed, gave way with it.

The one question the verse raises is why so complete a field-victory was not pressed home into a siege. Keil and Delitzsch suggest that it was already too late in the year, as may be gathered from ch. 11:1, which dates the siege of Rabbah to the return of spring. Charles Ellicott canvasses the possibilities without deciding — perhaps the army was too exhausted by its victory, perhaps unprovided with the apparatus of a siege, perhaps the season was too far advanced — and observes, with an eye to what follows, that the delay gave the allies opportunity to rally.

That closing observation is the hinge of the chapter. The witnesses do not divide over the substance; they simply note that the first campaign ends in a clean but incomplete triumph, the enemy beaten in the open but not yet subdued, the war interrupted rather than finished. It is a temptation to soldiers, the older commentators remark, to fly when they have a city at their backs to fly to; and it is the Syrians' wounded pride, not the Ammonites' safety behind their gate, that will reopen the war. Joab goes home to Jerusalem with a victory; the reader is quietly told to expect a second and greater battle before the matter is done.
15:
The beaten Syrians will not accept defeat, and the verse notes simply that when they saw that they were smitten before Israel, they gathered themselves together. The witnesses read it as the obstinacy of a wounded cause. John Gill pictures them turning the defeat over in their minds and talking it among themselves, dreading its consequences and resolving to try again. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the resolve a parable. This is a new attempt to recover their lost honour and to check the progress of David's victorious arms; and, he observes, even the baffled cause will make head as long as there is any life in it — as the enemies of the Son of David do, rallying after every rout, as the Pharisees gathered again against Christ (Matt. 22:34) and the kings of the earth muster for their last stand (Rev. 19:19).

Henry presses the folly of it. Conscious of their own insufficiency, the Syrians called in reinforcements from beyond the river (as v.16 will relate) and hoped so to make their part good against Israel; but they knew not the thoughts of the Lord, who was even then gathering them, as sheaves are gathered into the threshing-floor, precisely that they might be beaten (Mic. 4:12). Their regrouping, which they meant for their recovery, was in the divine reckoning their assembling for judgment — the very gathering of strength that would make their overthrow at Helam the more complete.

The witnesses do not divide; the single Reformed and Puritan voices agree, and the verse's brevity sharpens its lesson. There is a persistence that is not courage but hardness — a refusal to read the plain verdict of providence — and the second Syrian muster is its very type. What looks, from the plain of Aram, like a prudent regathering of strength is, from heaven, the drawing together of an enemy for a heavier fall. The chapter thus opens its final movement not with Israel's aggression but with Aram's proud relapse, and prepares the reader for the greater battle at Helam, in which the whole strength of Syria, and David himself, will take the field.
16:
The regathering finds a leader: Hadarezer sends and brings out the Syrians from beyond the river, and they come to Helam with Shobach, the captain of his host, at their head. The witnesses fix the names and the scale. Adam Clarke identifies the river as the Euphrates and notes that Hadarezer is the same king called Hadadezer in ch. 8:3, the two forms differing only by the easily confused Hebrew letters resh and daleth. John Gill reminds us that this Hadarezer stood at the head of the whole Syrian confederacy, the other kings being his servants. Keil and Delitzsch carry the muster to Helam under Shobach's command.

Charles Ellicott reads the verse as the measure of the first defeat's severity: Hadarezer felt the importance of the blow he had sustained, and now made a supreme effort to rally all his forces, summoning even his vassal tribes from beyond the Euphrates. Ellicott weighs the word Helam, granting that the Hebrew need not be a proper name and might mean simply their host, but since it plainly occurs as a place in v.17 he takes it as a name here too, an unlocated spot somewhere toward the Euphrates and not far from Hamath, which the Chronicler omits altogether. The uncertainty of the site does not touch the point of the verse — that the theatre of war has shifted decisively northward, from the plains of Ammon to the marches of the Euphrates.

The witnesses do not dispute the substance. They agree that the war has now enlarged from a border quarrel with Ammon and its nearer mercenaries into a trial of strength with the great Aramaean power east of the Euphrates, under its most formidable king and his named general. What began with an insult to David's envoys has drawn out the full might of Syria; and the very greatness of the gathering, the commentators quietly imply, only sets the stage for the more signal overthrow to come. The Syrians who fled from Joab now return in their whole strength — and by returning, deliver themselves up to a defeat from which, as the chapter will close, they will not recover through all the rest of David's reign.
17:
The renewed and enlarged threat draws David himself into the field. When it was told him, he gathered all Israel together, passed over Jordan, and came to Helam, where the Syrians set themselves in array and fought with him. The witnesses read the verse for the significance of the king's personal command. Adam Clarke states it simply: David gathered all Israel together because he judged that such a war — no longer a raid but a reckoning with the whole Aramaean confederacy — required his own presence. John Gill supplies the occasion, that word was brought to David of the Syrians' preparations and of where they lay, so that he did not wait for them but marched to meet them, crossing the Jordan to give battle on their own side of the river; the muster of all Israel, and not a detachment, shows how gravely the enlarged war was now taken.

Charles Ellicott draws out the disposition of the two theatres of war. Hearing of the great Syrian rally, David now took the field in person against the northern danger, while Joab — who had led the first campaign — was more probably left to the south, holding the Ammonites in check and preventing them from forming a junction with their confederates. The two enemies who had once fought as one are thus kept apart, each faced by its own Israelite force, so that the coalition can never again catch Israel between two fronts as it had at Medeba.

The witnesses do not divide; the verse is the deliberate advance of a king who will not delegate a decisive battle. Where in the coming chapter David's remaining at home while the army fought will be the occasion of his fall (ch. 11:1), here he is found at the head of his people, crossing Jordan to face the gathered strength of Syria himself. The tradition lets the contrast stand unspoken but plain: this is David at his best, personally bearing the burden of the nation's defence, and the field of Helam is set for the crushing victory that the next verse records.
18:
The great battle at Helam ends in a rout: the Syrians flee before Israel, and David destroys the men of seven hundred chariots and forty thousand horsemen, and smites Shobach their captain, who dies on the field. The witnesses read the verse for the completeness of the victory and for the well-known problem of its numbers. John Gill marks that the flight came only after an obstinate and bloody fight, and Keil and Delitzsch record that the general Shobach was so wounded that he did not survive the day.

The numbers, however, differ from the parallel account, and here the grammatical-historical care shows itself — though it is the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke who states the scribal solution most fully. Where this verse has seven hundred chariots, 1 Chron. 19:18 has seven thousand; and, Clarke explains, the ancient Hebrews often wrote numbers not in words but by numeral letters, and the letter for seven thousand and the letter for seven hundred are so alike that the one could easily be mistaken for the other — a slip that would account for many such discrepancies in these books. Charles Ellicott judges the smaller figure the more credible, since seven thousand chariots would be an almost incredible number, and reconciles the other variation — forty thousand horsemen here, forty thousand footmen in Chronicles — by supposing that each writer names only one arm of a force that in fact comprised both infantry and cavalry.

Beyond the arithmetic the witnesses agree on the weight of the day. Ellicott calls it a crushing blow, from which the Syrian power did not recover through the rest of David's reign or his son's. The mercenaries who had twice been hired against Israel, and the great king who had summoned them from beyond the Euphrates, are broken together; their general lies dead; and the field is cleared. What the coalition had meant as the recovery of its honour becomes instead the ruin of its strength, and the stage is set for the submission that closes the chapter. The commentators do not divide over the textual details; they treat them soberly, as marks of honest transmission, and let the substance stand: a decisive, God-given overthrow of the gathered might of Aram.
19:
The chapter closes with the fruit of the victory: all the kings who were servants to Hadarezer, seeing themselves smitten before Israel, make peace and become subject, and the Syrians fear to help the children of Ammon any more. The witnesses read the verse both as history fulfilled and as prophecy of what is to come. John Gill identifies the kings as the vassals subject to Hadarezer, at his beck and hired into his service, who now transfer their allegiance. Charles Ellicott qualifies the submission with care: it is the vassal kings who become tributary, and though it is not expressly said that Hadarezer himself did so, the loss of his forces (ch. 8:3) and the later reach of Solomon's realm to the Euphrates (1 Kings 4:21) make it probable that he too acknowledged David's suzerainty.

The wesleyan-arminian voices lift the outcome to the plane of promise. John Wesley sees here at length the fulfilment of the word given to Abraham and repeated to Joshua, that the borders of Israel should reach to the river Euphrates (Gen. 15:18; Josh. 1:4). Adam Clarke, with Dr. Delaney, counts this the last of eight wars in which David, over some nineteen or twenty years, was great, glorious, and pious — every war righteously undertaken and honourably ended.

Yet both Wesley and Clarke close on a note of warning that turns the reader toward the next chapter. Wesley draws the type: as Hanun entreated David's ambassadors shamefully, so the greater Son of David would send his apostles and ministers to the Jewish church, who mocked them, abused them, slew them, and so filled the measure of their iniquity and brought upon themselves ruin without remedy. And Clarke, having crowned David's glory, adds the shadow that the whole book is about to unfold: this splendid epoch is the very point at which the splendour begins to be obscured by crimes of the most flagitious nature — let him that most assuredly standeth take heed lest he fall (1 Cor. 10:12). The witnesses do not divide; they let the chapter end where it stands on the page — at the summit of David's outward triumph, with the peace of the nations secured and the promise to the fathers fulfilled, even as the reader is quietly braced for the fall that waits in the chapter to come.
